summary: Gruber responds to Ian Betteridge's attempt to use a 1997 Steve Jobs quote about Apple and Microsoft to counter Gruber's criticism of Google. He argues the Jobs quote was specific to its context — Apple was struggling and Jobs was delivering bitter medicine about letting go of the Apple-vs-Windows war, not claiming Apple could succeed without anyone losing. Gruber's core argument is that Google is just as ruthlessly competitive as Microsoft ever was, but disguises it behind a whimsical, utopian corporate persona. He contends that Apple and Microsoft at least owned their competitive nature, while Google pretends to be above the fray even as it enters every major tech market. The piece culminates in the charge that Google's defining flaw is hypocrisy, not competition itself.
key_points: Jobs's 1997 quote was bitter medicine delivered to a hostile audience, not a feel-good platitude — he was booed for surrendering the Apple-vs-Microsoft war
Larry Page's I/O message about Google not competing against others was applauded because it told the audience what they wanted to hear, making it fundamentally different from Jobs's 1997 moment
Google has made enemies of every major tech giant — Apple, Microsoft, Yahoo, Oracle, Facebook, Twitter, Amazon — while presenting itself as a peaceful techno-utopian company
The difference between Google and Microsoft's predatory behavior is that Microsoft owned and celebrated its competitiveness, while Google hides behind a whimsical facade
Gruber's complaint isn't that Google competes ruthlessly, but that it pretends it doesn't
money_quotes: The drum I'm trying to bang here is not that Google is a greedy competitor, but rather that Google is a greedy competitor that presents itself as anything but — as a sort of peaceful, whimsical, happy-go-lucky techno-futurist corporate utopian — and that rather than see this pose as absurd, many people, Googlers and Google users alike, buy it.
What major tech giant has Google not pitted itself against? Whose mashed potatoes do they not seek to take?
The difference between Google's predatory rapaciousness today and Microsoft's of yore is that Microsoft wore it on their sleeve, they owned it, celebrated it.
What rankles about Google is their hypocrisy.
Whereas Larry Page's pablum regarding Google not being pitted against other companies is farcical.
Page was telling the I/O audience what they wanted to hear, that Google is something other than a ruthless, greedy competitor.
If they're not the absolute truth, they're at least truthy.
key_insight: Google's sin isn't ruthless competition — every tech giant does that — but the hypocrisy of wrapping that ruthlessness in a veneer of whimsical corporate utopianism.
